text:
Here is one: the concept of convergent evolution.

Unrelated lineages, separated by vast stretches of time and geography, independently arrive at the same solution. Eyes evolved dozens of times across the animal kingdom. Wings emerged separately in insects, birds, and bats. Dolphins and ichthyosaurs, despite being separated by hundreds of millions of years and one being a mammal and the other a reptile, look nearly identical because they faced identical problems.

The strangeness is this: it suggests the solution space is not infinite. Nature is not exploring endlessly in all directions. It keeps finding the same answers. The same shapes. The same structures.

This implies something about the universe that is hard to articulate. Problems have optimal solutions. And given enough time and pressure, different systems will locate those solutions independently. The universe is, in some sense, guiding things toward specific configurations.

What I find beautiful about it is the implication for minds. Human cultures separated by oceans invented agriculture, mathematics, and music independently. Children who have never been taught certain logical principles reinvent them. Blind people who have never seen visual art produce drawings that follow standard perspective conventions.

If minds also converge, it suggests that reasoning is not arbitrary. That there are truths which any sufficiently developed intelligence will tend to find, not because it was taught them, but because the truths themselves exert a kind of pressure.

It makes the universe feel less random. Not because something is directing it, but because the landscape of possible answers has a shape. And that shape is real, and discoverable, and apparently not unique to us.

That is beautiful to me.
